current_fact.fact_text: In 1963, Project Mercury came to a close with Gordon Cooper’s flight, capping the United States’ first crewed space program that had begun just five years earlier. Mercury proved that humans could survive and work in orbit, turning what was once science fiction into a repeatable, almost routine reality.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

current_fact.match_reason: A 'destination' beyond Earth—Project Mercury’s leap into orbit—pairs with an episode about voyaging into new territories, complete with spacey tracks like 'Breathe'.

queue.fact_text: On a more symbolic level, the 1850 ratification of the Arana–Southern Treaty showed that even in an era of gunboat diplomacy, trade routes and river mouths could be negotiated back from imperial powers. It’s a reminder that the flow of goods, culture, and music has always depended on who controls the waterways.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A treaty over river navigation and trade echoes an episode built around boat journeys and coastal music, all about who gets to travel—and how.

published.fact_text: In 1963, astronaut Gordon Cooper flew Mercury-Atlas 9, the final mission of Project Mercury, becoming the first American to spend more than a full day in space and the last American to orbit Earth entirely alone. His 22-orbit mission tested human endurance, onboard navigation, and the limits of early space technology, setting the stage for the Gemini and Apollo programs.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal 'space shift': Cooper’s solitary orbiting of Earth mirrors the episode’s theme of reimagining emptiness as creative space.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1997, Space Shuttle Atlantis launched on STS-84 to dock with the Russian space station Mir, part of a series of joint missions that bridged two former Cold War rivals. The flight ferried supplies, scientific experiments, and a new crew member, turning low Earth orbit into one of humanity’s first truly international laboratories.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: An orbital rendezvous between American and Russian crews matches a mix that travels seamlessly between cultures, orbiting the globe through sound.

published.fact_text: In 1618, Johannes Kepler finally trusted his own calculations and confirmed his third law of planetary motion, which links a planet’s orbital period to the size of its orbit. The idea had seemed so elegant that he initially rejected it as suspiciously neat, a reminder that even geniuses sometimes doubt their most beautiful insights.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: Kepler’s reluctant acceptance of his own law is a perfect example of the messy, joyful learning curve explored in this reflective episode.

published.fact_text: During an annular solar eclipse in 1836, English astronomer Francis Baily noticed a strange glittering effect as sunlight streamed through lunar valleys, creating a string of bright beads along the Moon’s edge. These 'Baily’s beads' became a classic example of how tiny surface details on distant worlds can create breathtaking phenomena in our sky.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the eclipse beads and Arctic whale encounters turn subtle light and shadow into awe, mirrored by the episode’s cinematic, nature-soaked atmosphere.

published.fact_text: On a remote Pacific atoll in 1957, Britain detonated its first hydrogen bomb during Operation Grapple, leaping into the thermonuclear age with a blast whose power was measured in megatons. The test turned a seemingly idyllic coral island into a symbol of Cold War brinkmanship and the invisible scars of nuclear experimentation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The sudden, overwhelming 'shock' of a hydrogen blast parallels the episode’s high-voltage guitar energy and its title, 'Electric Shock'.

published.fact_text: In 1940, the submarine USS Squalus was raised, repaired, and quietly returned to service as USS Sailfish, a rare case of a sunken vessel resurrected and sent back into war. The boat carried the memory of a dramatic pre-war rescue and went on to earn nine battle stars in the Pacific, a second life forged from a near disaster.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A submarine literally raised from the depths fits an episode built around immersion, water, and resurfacing with new perspective.

published.fact_text: The 1850 Arana–Southern Treaty formally ended a tense standoff between Great Britain and Argentina, after British and French navies had tried to force open the Paraná River for trade. The agreement quietly acknowledged Argentina’s control over its inland waters, an early diplomatic recognition of a South American nation’s sovereignty over its own river routes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A riverine sovereignty dispute resolved by negotiation echoes the episode’s theme of travel, shifting perspectives, and finding calmer currents instead of conflict.

published.fact_text: In 2001, a CSX freight train led by locomotive 8888 slipped away from its crew in Ohio and sped south for 66 miles without a driver, hauling tank cars full of hazardous chemicals. The runaway train, eventually stopped by a pursuing locomotive, became so famous it inspired the Hollywood film 'Unstoppable'.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A train that literally goes 'on the go' without stopping fits the kinetic, forward-rolling travel energy of this On The Go episode.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1725, Johann Sebastian Bach led the first performance of his cantata 'Ich bin ein guter Hirt' (BWV 85), a contemplative piece about Jesus as the Good Shepherd. The work weaves intimate vocal lines and gentle instrumentation into a musical meditation on care, guidance, and trust.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

published.match_reason: A baroque cantata that has outlived centuries belongs with an episode celebrating 'timeless' music and the long legacy of soulful sound.

published.fact_text: In 1602, English navigator Bartholomew Gosnold became one of the first Europeans to sight and name Cape Cod, reportedly inspired by the abundance of codfish in its waters. His short exploratory voyage helped map the New England coastline, laying quiet groundwork for later colonization.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: An early Atlantic coastal voyage fits neatly with an episode literally recorded at sea, full of stories from life aboard a sailing boat.

published.fact_text: In 2010, Australian teenager Jessica Watson sailed back into Sydney Harbour after spending 210 days alone at sea, becoming the youngest person to complete a solo, non-stop, unassisted circumnavigation of the globe. She weathered storms, equipment failures, and long stretches of isolation, turning the open ocean into both classroom and home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: Watson’s disciplined, ocean-bound journey resonates with an episode about physical training, persistence, and the sea breeze rhythms of coastal Thailand.

published.fact_text: In 1970, Anna Mae Hays and Elizabeth P. Hoisington shattered a military glass ceiling when they became the first female generals in United States Army history. Their promotions acknowledged decades of service by women in uniform, especially in nursing and support roles often overlooked in official narratives.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

published.match_reason: Trailblazing women in the army align with an episode centering powerful female voices in hip hop, all about women claiming space and authority.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1948, as the British Mandate for Palestine expired, armies from Egypt, Transjordan, Lebanon, Syria, Iraq, and Saudi Arabia entered the newly declared State of Israel, igniting the 1948 Arab–Israeli War. The conflict reshaped borders, displaced hundreds of thousands, and still reverberates in Middle Eastern politics and identities.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A formative Middle Eastern war and its long shadows pair with music born of desert landscapes, exile, and contested homelands.

published.fact_text: Also in 1940, during the German invasion of the Netherlands, outgunned and underprepared Dutch forces surrendered after only five days of fighting, ushering in five years of occupation. The rapid collapse, punctuated by the devastating bombing of Rotterdam, left a deep imprint on Dutch memory and post-war reconstruction.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A story of rapid defeat followed by years of rebuilding parallels an episode about storms, recovery, and the reassurance that 'it can’t rain forever'.

published.fact_text: In 1933, Nazi Germany quietly merged all aviation organizations under the Ministry of Aviation, forming the core of a new air arm that would soon be known as the Luftwaffe. The move was technically illegal under the Treaty of Versailles, but it signaled a rapid militarization of the skies that would define the coming war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

published.match_reason: An industrialized air force built from factories and bureaucracy resonates with an episode centered on 'Steel City' sounds and the machinery of modern life.

published.fact_text: In 1911, during the Mexican Revolution, revolutionary forces under Emilio Madero captured the city of Torreón—but in the chaos that followed, more than 300 Chinese residents were massacred. The episode, often overlooked, reveals how racism and scapegoating can erupt violently even within broader struggles for justice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A brutal, racially charged massacre within a revolution fits an episode about political rage, protest music, and confronting systemic violence.

published.fact_text: The first major Australian gold rush was officially proclaimed in 1851, months after gold had quietly been discovered near Bathurst in New South Wales. News of the find triggered a wave of prospectors who reshaped Australia’s economy, demographics, and even its environmental landscape in a few short decades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: Just as gold rushes transformed societies through a sudden resource boom, Afrobeat’s rise reshaped musical economies with a new, rich 'gold' of rhythm.

published.fact_text: In 1567, Mary, Queen of Scots, married James Hepburn, Earl of Bothwell—who was widely suspected of orchestrating the murder of her previous husband, Lord Darnley. The scandalous union shocked Europe, eroded her support at home, and set Mary on a path that would end in imprisonment and execution.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

published.match_reason: A dangerous, politically catastrophic marriage suits an episode about irrational love, vulnerability, and the complicated edges of romance.

published.fact_text: In 1532, the English clergy signed the Submission of the Clergy, formally conceding that they could no longer make church laws without the consent of King Henry VIII. This quiet legal document helped sever England’s church from Rome and laid the groundwork for the Church of England as a state-controlled institution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

published.match_reason: A kingdom forcing its church to 'think again' about authority fits an episode explicitly about unlearning, shifting beliefs, and challenging tradition.

published.fact_text: In 1525, insurgent peasants inspired by radical preacher Thomas Müntzer were decisively crushed at the Battle of Frankenhausen, effectively ending the German Peasants' War. What began as a call for social and spiritual reform turned into a brutal reminder of how harshly entrenched powers can respond to popular uprisings.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

published.match_reason: A failed peasant revolution led by a fiery preacher pairs with an episode about Residente, whose art channels political resistance and the costs of speaking out.

published.fact_text: The British Mandate for Palestine officially ended in 1948, closing a three-decade chapter in which Britain administered a region promised in conflicting ways to both Jews and Arabs. Its expiration created a sudden political vacuum into which competing national movements—and neighboring armies—rushed, sparking war and redrawing maps.

episode.title: Morning Drops #02 - Waves of the Maghreb
episode.description: Oriental vibes guided by Yasmin. Discovering curious facts about the Maghreb, together with its fascinating music! A wonderful trip with a selection of songs from North Africa.
songs: Nass El Ghiwane - El Madi Fate

published.match_reason: A mandate ending in the Eastern Mediterranean is tightly connected to broader North African and Arab histories, echoed by an episode steeped in Maghreb sounds and stories.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1836, British astronomer Francis Baily made his famous observation of 'Baily’s beads' during an annular eclipse, helping to popularize detailed eclipse expeditions among scientists and the public. His vivid description of the shimmering light sparked new fascination with carefully observing transient celestial events.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: An invitation to slow down and watch an eclipse fits the episode’s gentle 'Slow Planet' mood—lingering with the sky instead of rushing past it.

published.fact_text: In 1963, Project Mercury came to a close with Gordon Cooper’s flight, capping the United States’ first crewed space program that had begun just five years earlier. Mercury proved that humans could survive and work in orbit, turning what was once science fiction into a repeatable, almost routine reality.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)
